title: Real-World Inpainting Use Cases
concept: Inpainting powers object removal, photo restoration, and creative content extension in real production tools. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Image Editing with Diffusion.'

exercises.prompt: You take a photo but a stranger walks into the background. What do you wish you could do?

options.text: Magically erase the stranger and fill in the background
Draw a mustache on the stranger

exercises.explain: That 'magic erase and fill' is exactly what inpainting does in real apps!

cards.title: From Lab Trick to Everyday Tool
cards.body: Inpainting isn't just a cool AI demo anymore. It's inside your phone's photo editor, movie studios, museums, and design apps — quietly fixing, removing, and extending images every single day.

cards.title: Real-World Inpainting
cards.body: Inpainting is used in real products to: 1) remove unwanted objects from photos, 2) restore old or damaged pictures, and 3) extend images beyond their original edges (like adding more sky or background). The AI fills in new pixels that blend naturally with the rest.

cards.title: The Invisible Tailor
cards.body: Think of inpainting like a tailor patching a torn jacket. A skilled tailor doesn't just slap on any patch — they match the color, pattern, and texture so you can't even tell it was torn. Inpainting AI does this with pixels instead of fabric.

cards.title: Three Everyday Uses
cards.body: 1) Phone apps let you tap a photobomber and erase them completely. 2) Museums use AI to restore faded or torn historical photographs and paintings. 3) Designers use 'generative fill' to stretch a small image into a wide banner by inventing new matching background.

cards.title: Myth: It's Only for Fun Filters
cards.body: Myth: Inpainting is just a silly camera filter trick. Truth: It's used professionally in film production (removing wires/rigs from shots), forensic and historical photo restoration, e-commerce (cleaning up product photos), and even satellite image repair.

cards.title: Old vs New Photo Fixing
cards.body: See how inpainting changes the way we repair and extend images compared to old manual methods.

left.text: Manual retouching pixel by pixel, hours of skilled human editing

right.text: AI fills gaps in seconds, learning patterns to match texture and lighting

exercises.prompt: Which is a real-world use case of inpainting?

options.text: Removing a photobomber from a vacation picture

exercises.explain: Object removal from photos is one of the most common real-world inpainting uses.

exercises.prompt: Inpainting can be used to restore old, damaged photographs.
exercises.explain: True! Museums and archives use inpainting to fix torn, faded, or scratched historical images.

exercises.sentence: Designers use inpainting to ___ a small image into a wider banner by generating new matching background.
exercises.explain: Inpainting can extend images beyond their original borders, a use called 'generative fill' or outpainting.

exercises.prompt: A movie studio wants to remove a camera crew's shadow accidentally caught in a scene. What tool would help most?

options.text: Inpainting to fill the shadow area with matching background

exercises.explain: Inpainting is exactly how film studios remove unwanted objects like shadows, wires, or equipment from footage.

exercises.prompt: A photo app announces a new feature: 'Tap any object to erase it, and we'll seamlessly fill the space using AI.' What technology powers this feature, and why does it work so well?

options.text: Inpainting — because the AI learned patterns from millions of images and can generate matching textures and colors to fill gaps
Simple copy-paste of a random image from the internet
Manual editing done instantly by a human artist
Basic color inversion of the photo

exercises.explain: This is inpainting in action — used in phone apps, film, restoration, and design to remove, fix, and extend images realistically. Next up: Image Editing with Diffusion.
